verdict.headline: The 65-fix campaign did NOT cleanly hold — it spawned a tight cluster of case/prefix/name-vs-id regressions (5 confirmed, 4 high-severity) all in the freshly-touched PR-spec and workflow-trajectory code — while AXON's deepest invariant (axon/ write-protection + the kernel-change floor) remains genuinely hard and the tool surface is healthy.
verdict.position: AXON on merged main (kernel v1.1.5) is two systems wearing one badge. The deep enforcement spine is real and verified: axon/ write-protection is triple-wired (in-process R9WriteError before disk, enforce.py exit 1, PreToolUse hook exit 2 — all live), the kernel-change floor is structurally never-delegable (denied before the grant even loads, double-filtered at grant time, content-hash locked to baa26d9a...e16a2 which matches the merged file byte-for-byte), the destructive override is default-off and per-op-per-repo scoped, and ~45 sampled ACTIVE tools all run with real output (health 135 ACTIVE/0 FAILED, registry 147/147 zero drift). That is a strong floor and it is the hardest thing AXON claims. But the campaign that fixed 65 bugs introduced a tight regression cluster in exactly the code it touched, and the regressions share ONE root cause: an unresolved PR-spec filename convention war (uppercase-zero-padded `PR-001.md` as written by the producers vs lowercase/unpadded `pr-N.md` assumed by consumers) plus a name-vs-id field mismatch in workflow trajectories. The fixes were applied to ONE site each and their siblings left unfixed (drift fixed, export+ready not; promote records NAME, advance compares ID). Worse, three of these fixes are inverted or mutually incompatible: the freeze/undo fix moved the stuck-frozen bug from project-meta to phase-meta and shipped a regression test that validates only the producer and never asserts undo restores both files (its own docstring admits undo reverses one row); the pr-create status-flip fix double-prefixes to `## PR-PR-001` and is a guaranteed no-op re-introducing the exact symptom it targeted; the workflow promote-fix and skip-detection-fix cannot both be on — wiring the guard breaks promote, leaving promote on breaks the guard. Net: 392+ area tests are green over divergent state because the tests lock the wrong behavior. Outside the regressions, the per-turn enforcement story is honestly self-conceded as advisory (the Stop hook is LOG-ONLY by design — coherence/identity/rules are computed and surfaced but cannot stop a render), the headline axon-million proof is harness-ready but has zero collected results, and compiled .cmp.md artifacts have no content-hash guard (byte-identical regen explicitly deferred) so they can silently drift. The position is: solid floor, healthy tools, honest about its advisory ceiling — but the most recent campaign regressed the PR/workflow surface and the green test suite is actively masking it.
verdict.fix_campaign_held: NO — not cleanly. 5 confirmed regressions of the 65, 4 of them high-severity, all clustered in the just-touched PR-spec + workflow-trajectory code. Three are the worst failure mode — a fix that LOCKS the wrong behavior with a passing test: (1) freeze/undo — fix inverted the bug (phase-meta now stuck frozen instead of project-meta); the regression test test_safety_freeze_undo_logging.py validates only the producer (both metas snapshotted, distinct ids) and NEVER asserts a single `code-dev undo` restores both — its docstring at lines 142-143 admits undo reverses LAST/one-by-id, so it knowingly passes a still-divergent state; (2) pr-create status-flip — the super-polish #40 fix interpolates section=\"## PR-{pr-id}\" → \"## PR-PR-001\" (double prefix; line 98 in the SAME file does it correctly) → guaranteed no-op, PR stays not-started forever, the exact symptom #40 targeted; (3) advance-guard — the promote fix records the synapse NAME (line 120) while advance() compares last-node to cursor.id (workflow_run.py:79); the two same-campaign fixes are structurally incompatible — skip-detection is dead in-program (no --run-id at line 179) AND broken on the CLI path. Plus the unfixed siblings of an already-fixed bug: pr-export (builds lowercase pr-{n}.md, crashes with uncaught FileNotFoundError — the existence guard added to pr_drift was NOT mirrored) and pr-ready (builds double-prefixed pr-PR-007.md → --strict governance gate evaluates a missing file). I verified all 5 against source line-by-line; every cited line and mechanism is exact. The campaign hardened the kernel/safety floor (those held) but regressed the PR + workflow feature surface, and the test suite is masking it.
verdict.new_issue_counts: critical:0 high:4 medium:5 low:1 (of NEW non-regression confirmed bugs). NEW high: pr-create PR-PR-001 no-op; plan_dag empty-DAG (lowercase glob misses uppercase specs → ok:true empty DAG for every real project); workflow-run --name crash at first transition (empty W:_workflow-run-path); advance trajectory name/id mismatch (WorkflowJumpError on first transition whenever a trajectory is supplied). NEW medium: pr-review P9 status never flips (cross-line + case-wrong regex); journal-log unbound pr-id on general entries; thaw corrupts project workflow-step to phase's value + dangling `# was:` comment; workflow-new resume loses all built state (PHASE-C resets synapses, PHASE-D/E read unhydrated locals); W:ws-path never set so trajectory writes off-tree; R_MEMORY_RESPECTED manifest BLOCK degrades to WARN unless CWD is repo root (state:{} vs siblings get repo_root). NEW low: crucible change-set gate fail-open (base→literal 'HEAD~1' → 0 violations, vacuous pass) on single-commit/no-origin repos. SEPARATELY (counted in regressions above, not here): the 5 regressions of the 65 add 4 more high. Two recurring root-cause families dominate: (A) the PR-spec filename case/pad war — touches pr-export, pr-ready, pr-create, plan_dag, pr-review; (B) the workflow name-vs-id / unset-W-key family — touches advance-guard x2, --name crash, ws-path off-tree, workflow-new resume.
strengths: axon/ write-protection (R9) is genuinely hard and triple-wired — verified LIVE: _axon_io.atomic_write('axon/__probe__') raises R9WriteError before touching disk, enforce.py check-write exits 1, PreToolUse hook (now installed + active in settings.json) exits 2. This is the one marquee claim where 'enforced' really means 'a program cannot bypass it.'

kernel-change floor is structurally inviolable — denied in autonomous_mode.py:110-111 BEFORE load_grant() (no grant state can reach it), double-filtered at grant time; kernel content sha256-locked to baa26d9a...e16a2 and the merged file matches EXACTLY, so the safety prose cannot drift silently. test_kernel_change_never_delegable + version-lock pass.

Destructive override is default-OFF, per-op AND per-repo scoped, and audited (grant-on/authorized/authorized-destructive records). The floor holds until an op is explicitly opted in per repo. Verified empirically.

Tool surface is healthy and live — ~45 ACTIVE tools sampled across every category run with real output (dag, phase-model, workflow-runner traversal guard, cron, dispatch, verify, crucible, autonomous-mode, pr_* family, reservoir-pvt, project-graph). health.py=135 ACTIVE/0 FAILED, registry_drift=147/147 zero drift, no dead ACTIVE tools found.

crucible/merge gate is fail-CLOSED — errored/unknown control counts as BLOCK; manifest.py + test_rules_manifest lock the 4 runner lists so a rule can't silently go dead. The hard-block enforcement that DOES exist sits correctly at the merge gate.

Kernel self-honesty is admirable — KERNEL-SLIM 'Enforcement reality' (lines 89-95) openly concedes the per-turn response gate is advisory/agent-discipline, tells the reader to run verify.py status and NOT read 'enforced' as 'cannot be bypassed.' It under-claims its now-installed write-gate rather than over-claiming.

dont-do enforcement is largely coherent end-to-end — write-time token block (exit 2) + change-set R_DONT_DO (fail-closed prose/semantic BLOCK) + capture-lint, all wired into crucible, all fail-SAFE (a hook fault never blocks legit work), 103 related tests pass.
top_gaps: The PR-spec filename convention is unsettled and the producers/consumers disagree (uppercase-padded PR-001.md written; lowercase pr-N.md read in export/ready/plan_dag/pr-review). This ONE unresolved convention is the root cause of 5+ bugs and 3 of the 4 high-severity regressions. Until the convention is locked with a shared helper + a test, this family will keep regressing.

The test suite is masking regressions — 392+ area tests green over divergent freeze/undo state because the regression test validates only the producer; pr-create no-op ships green; plan_dag returns ok:true on an EMPTY DAG for every real project. Green does not currently mean correct on the feature surface. Missing: end-to-end assertions on observed final state (does undo restore BOTH files? did the status line actually flip? does the DAG have nodes?).

The workflow trajectory subsystem has a structural name-vs-id contradiction baked in by two same-campaign fixes that cannot coexist (promote needs NAME, skip-guard compares ID). Skip-detection — billed as 'the real teeth' — is dead in-program and broken on the CLI. Plus --name mode crashes at the first transition and trajectories write off-tree (W:ws-path unset). The whole advance-guard + trajectory path is effectively non-functional as shipped.

Per-turn enforcement is advisory-only by design — the sole per-turn surface (Stop hook) is LOG-ONLY, so coherence/identity/rules are computed and surfaced but cannot stop a render. The 'proactive / before render / HALT' temporal claim is FALSE as wired. Honest in the kernel note, but it means 4 of 5 marquee claims are advisory in the live session.

The headline axon-million 'AXON beats bare model' (H1) claim has ZERO collected results — benchmark/reports/ holds only .gitkeep, dual_agent_eval returns status:'harness-ready', pre-registration locked but unexecuted. The flagship proof is unproven and owner-gated on an API key.

Compiled .cmp.md artifacts can silently drift — byte-identical regen-and-diff CI gate explicitly DEFERRED, headers carry a wall-clock timestamp and NO source content-hash, only a structural freshness check exists; several .cmp.md are orphans with no source program at all.

Multiple fail-open seams in the static gate: crucible change-set returns 0 violations (vacuous pass) when the diff base can't resolve (single-commit/no-origin → literal 'HEAD~1'), and R_MEMORY_RESPECTED's manifest-declared BLOCK silently degrades to WARN unless invoked from exactly the repo root (state:{} while its three sibling rules in the same loop get repo_root). Same fail-open class the C10 fix was meant to close.

git-op guarantees are advisory at the tool boundary — authorized() has NO production caller (only its own CLI 'check'); a confused agent that just runs `git push --force` is not mechanically blocked by this layer. Plus the delete-branch (safe default) vs branch-delete (destructive) naming collision auto-authorizes branch deletion whenever the caller spells it the safe way.
recommendations: FIRST — fix the 3 inverted/no-op regressions that ship green, because a passing test over wrong behavior is worse than a known-open bug: (a) pr-create.md:312 change section="## PR-{pr-id}" → "## {pr-id}" (match line 98); (b) freeze/undo — make `code-dev undo` loop over all rows sharing a base action-id (strip trailing letter), and ADD an end-to-end assertion that one undo restores BOTH metas; (c) decide promote-vs-skip-guard: either record BOTH name and id in the trajectory, or have advance() resolve name→id before comparing — then wire --run-id into run.md:179. These three are actively masked by the suite.

Lock the PR-spec filename convention ONCE with a single shared path helper (canonical = uppercase-zero-padded PR-NNN.md, matching every producer + every real file on disk) and route pr-export/pr-ready/plan_dag/pr-review/pr-drift through it. Add ONE test that asserts producer-written path == consumer-read path. This kills the largest bug family (5+ bugs) at the root and prevents the next regression. While there, mirror pr_drift's existence guard into pr_export.py:31 so a miss returns a result, not a traceback.

Add observed-final-state assertions to the highest-value flows, not just producer-side checks: plan_dag (assert nodes ≠ [] on a real project — would have caught the empty-DAG instantly), pr-review P9 / pr-create (assert the status line actually changed to the new value), freeze→undo→status (assert convergence), workflow --name (assert it survives the first transition). The current suite's blind spot is 'did the side effect actually happen,' which is exactly where every regression hid.

Repair the workflow trajectory subsystem as a unit (it is non-functional as shipped): STORE W:_workflow-run-path on the --name path, STORE W:ws-path (or default it to 'workspace') so trajectories land on-tree, and resolve the name/id mismatch. Until done, the advance-guard 'teeth' and skip-detection are dead — either fix or honestly mark the trajectory features as not-yet-wired so they aren't trusted.

Close the two fail-open seams in the static gate: make crucible change_files() fail-CLOSED (refuse, don't green-light, when the diff base can't resolve), and pass repo_root to r_memory_respected.check in the run_changeset loop (crucible.py:176) like its three siblings, so the manifest BLOCK doesn't silently become WARN off-root. Both are the same fail-open class the campaign already tried to close elsewhere.

Fix the lower-severity but easy wins while in these files: journal-log unbound pr-id at lines 166/310 (add the '| general' fallback already used at 183/211/257); thaw — parse project prior from the PROJECT meta (not phase) and fix the substring REPLACE pattern; workflow-new resume — rehydrate locals from state on the PHASE-D/E path and stop resetting synapses in PHASE-C; rename the delete-branch/branch-delete collision to ONE token.

Update the now-stale kernel/harness self-notes (the write-gate hedge in KERNEL-SLIM 89-95 and the STORE(L:host-cap-enforce,'self') target in claude-code.md) — the PreToolUse hook IS installed now, so the kernel currently UNDER-claims its real write-gate. Cheap honesty win that keeps the self-report trustworthy.

Decide explicitly on the advisory ceiling and the unproven proof — these are the two biggest 'promise vs reality' gaps, both currently honest-but-open. Either collect the axon-million baseline (owner-gated, needs the API key) or stop foregrounding H1 as a result; and either escalate the Stop hook toward exit-2 (after the false-positive data it asks for) or keep documenting the per-turn gate as advisory. Don't let 'harness-ready' and 'computed-but-advisory' quietly read as 'proven' and 'enforced.'

confirmed_findings.title: freeze→undo still leaves a _meta.md stuck 'frozen' (fix inverted the bug; its regression test locks the wrong behavior)
confirmed_findings.mechanism: The original Bug-16 fix was 'append a SECOND _actions.log entry for proj_meta, OR make undo restore every snapshot under one action-id'. The implementers did ONLY the first half: freeze now writes two rows — `{action-id}  replace  {phase-dir}/_meta.md  …/phase_meta.md` (line 58-59) and `{action-id}b  replace  {proj-dir}/_meta.md  …/proj_meta.md` (line 60-61). But code-dev-state-undo reverses exactly ONE row per invocation: `target ← IF sub ≠ ∅ → FIRST(FILTER(entries, contains=sub)) | LAST(entries)` (undo.md:55), then it parses that single row's op/target/snap (lines 59-63) and restores only it (lines 72-89). There is no loop over rows sharing a base action-id. A bare `code-dev undo` after a freeze picks `LAST(entries)` = the proj_meta row (`{action-id}b`, appended last), so it restores ONLY the project _meta.md and leaves the PHASE _meta.md at 'workflow-step: frozen  # was: …'. The bug is not fixed — it is inverted (phase meta now stuck instead of project meta); the post-undo state is still divergent, and `code-dev status`/resume still disagree. The regression test test_safety_freeze_undo_logging.py validates ONLY the producer side (both metas snapshotted; each snapshot has its own distinct-id _actions.log row) and never asserts that a single `code-dev undo` actually restores both files — its own comment at lines 142-143 admits 'Undo's default reverses LAST(entries); code-dev undo <id> targets one by id', i.e. it knows undo reverses one row yet still passes the fix as correct. So 392 area tests are green over a still-divergent state.
confirmed_findings.fix: Make undo restore every row sharing the base action-id in one pass: after selecting `target`, derive base-id = target's action-id with any trailing letter suffix stripped, then `group ← FILTER(entries, action-id-base ≡ base-id)` and reverse each row in the group (COPY-FILE per replace row). Update test_safety_freeze_undo_logging.py to assert the END STATE: simulate a freeze+undo and check BOTH _meta.md files are restored, not just that two distinct rows were logged.

confirmed_findings.title: pr-export reads the wrong-case spec filename → pr_export.py crashes with FileNotFoundError (sibling of an already-fixed bug, left unfixed)
confirmed_findings.mechanism: The original finding 'drift / export / pr-ready read the wrong spec filename (case + zero-pad mismatch); drift also crashes on the miss' was fixed ONLY for drift. code-dev-pr-create writes specs as `03-prs/PR-{pad(pr-n,3)}.md` (uppercase, zero-padded — PR-003.md; confirmed by fixtures PR-001.md…PR-100.md and create.md:97 `pr-id ← "PR-{pad(pr-n,3)}"`). code-dev-pr-drift.md:37 was corrected to `03-prs/PR-{pad(pr-num,3)}.md` and pr_drift.drift() got an existence guard (pr_drift.py:55-57 returns {ok:false,reason:'spec not found'}). But code-dev-pr-export.md:37 STILL builds `03-prs/pr-{pr-num}.md` (lowercase, no pad), so for PR 3 it builds `pr-3.md` while the real file is `PR-003.md`. Worse, pr_export.py:31 export() does `spec_path.read_text(...)` with NO existence guard (the guard added to pr_drift was NOT mirrored here), so the casing miss raises an uncaught FileNotFoundError and the export tool crashes instead of returning a result.
confirmed_findings.fix: In code-dev-pr-export.md:37 use the create convention: `spec-path ← "{proj-dir}/03-prs/PR-{pad(pr-num,3)}.md"` (mirroring pr-drift.md:37). Add an existence guard to pr_export.export(): `if not spec_path.is_file(): return {"ok": False, "reason": "spec not found", "spec": str(spec_path)}` before read_text(), mirroring pr_drift.py:55-57.

confirmed_findings.title: pr-ready --strict builds a double-prefixed, nonexistent spec path (pr-PR-007.md) → governance gate evaluates against a missing file
confirmed_findings.mechanism: Same original finding as #2's sibling; the pr-ready half was not fixed. pr-ready resolves the PR id as `pr ← RETRIEVE(W:code-dev-pr-create) | QUERY(user): "Which PR? (e.g. PR-007)"` (line 40), and preflight stores it via `STORE(W:code-dev-pr-create, pr)` where pr is the already-formatted id `PR-007`. Line 93 then builds `pr-spec ← "…/03-prs/pr-{W:code-dev-pr-create}.md"` = `03-prs/pr-PR-007.md` — a double `pr-` prefix that exists nowhere (the real file is `PR-007.md`). This `pr-spec` is passed as `--target` to `TOOL(rules, evaluate, …)` (lines 95-97), so the full --strict governance gate (R-rules over the spec) runs against a missing spec file: rules that read the spec see empty/absent content and the strict PASS/BLOCK verdict is meaningless. The original fix note explicitly said 'in pr-ready.md drop the extra pr- prefix'; it was applied to pr-drift but not here.
confirmed_findings.fix: Change line 93 to the create convention without the extra prefix: `pr-spec ← "{W:myaxon-dev-projects}/{W:code-dev-project}/03-prs/{W:code-dev-pr-create}.md"` (since the var already holds `PR-007`), or normalize-and-pad a numeric arg to `PR-{pad(n,3)}`. Match exactly what code-dev-pr-create writes (PR-00N.md).

confirmed_findings.title: advance-guard skip-detection ('the real teeth') is wired into the tool but unreachable from workflow-run, and is structurally incompatible with the promote fix
confirmed_findings.line: run.md:179 (advance call) + run.md:120 (record-step) vs workflow_run.py:70-83 (trajectory check) & 238-241 (CLI loads traj only with --run-id)
confirmed_findings.mechanism: The Bug-high fix 'advance-guard is structurally a no-op' added real teeth to advance(): when a `trajectory` is supplied it rejects a transition whose `from` ≠ the last-recorded node and requires the first step to equal `start` (workflow_run.py:70-83). The CLI activates this ONLY when `--run-id` is passed (line 239: `traj = load_trajectory(...) if args.run_id else None`). But workflow-run.md's advance call omits --run-id entirely: `TOOL(workflow-runner, advance, "--workflow … --from {cursor.id} --to {next-id}…")` (line 179) — no run-id, so traj is None and advance() falls straight through to the old single-edge check (line 84-92). The guard therefore remains the no-op the fix was meant to cure. Worse, the two fixes are mutually incompatible by construction: the promote fix records the trajectory by NAME (`record-step … --node {cursor.name}`, line 120) so promoted drafts stay executable, while advance()'s skip check compares the last trajectory node against `cursor.id` (line 79: `last != cursor_id`). Since ids are `s1/s2/…` and names are `code-dev-study/…`, wiring --run-id into line 179 would make `_last_node` return a NAME and advance() raise `out-of-order advance` on essentially every step. So skip-detection cannot be turned on without breaking promote — the capability is dead as shipped. run.md's own comment (lines 116-119) acknowledges the split ('Record the synapse NAME… The advance-guard below still keys off cursor.id').
confirmed_findings.fix: Record BOTH id and name in the trajectory (record_step already accepts name; change run.md:120 to keep --node {cursor.id} AND add --name {cursor.name}), make advance()'s skip check compare against the step's node-ID while promote() reads step['name'] (it already prefers name via _step_name). THEN wire --run-id {run-id} into the advance call at run.md:179 so the teeth actually engage. As-is, either keep them off (status quo) or the two fixes collide.

confirmed_findings.title: pr-create status-flip is a guaranteed no-op: section="## PR-{pr-id}" double-prefixes to "## PR-PR-001" and matches nothing (regression of super-polish #40)
confirmed_findings.mechanism: Line 97 sets pr-id ← "PR-{pad(pr-n,3)}" (e.g. PR-001). The super-polish fix for finding #40 then does at line 312: pr-block ← EXTRACT(prs-doc, section="## PR-{pr-id}"), which interpolates to section="## PR-PR-001". But code-dev-plan.md:309-310 writes the section header as "## {pr.number} — title" = "## PR-001" (pr.number already carries the PR- prefix). Every other section= call in the codebase passes the literal header text (e.g. section="## Summary"); there is no auto-prefixing. Line 98 in this very file already does it correctly: EXTRACT(prs-list, "## {pr-id}"). So the extract at 312 returns empty, REPLACE(pr-block,...) at 313 yields empty new-block, and REPLACE(02-prs.md, pr-block='', new-block='') at 314 is empty-with-empty = no-op. The PR row stays '- **Status:** not-started' forever — exactly the symptom finding #40 was meant to fix, re-introduced by the fix's literal double 'PR-' prefix.
confirmed_findings.fix: Change line 312 to use the same form as line 98: pr-block ← EXTRACT(prs-doc, section="## {pr-id}"). (Drop the literal 'PR-' prefix so the interpolated header is '## PR-001', matching what the planner writes.)

confirmed_findings.title: plan_dag.py emits an EMPTY DAG (ok:true) for every real project: glob('pr-*.md') is case-sensitive and misses the uppercase PR-NNN.md spec files that are actually written
confirmed_findings.mechanism: run() discovers PR specs via prs_dir.glob('pr-*.md') (lowercase). But code-dev-pr-create.md:264 writes specs as 'PR-{pad(pr-n,3)}.md' = PR-001.md (uppercase), and every real project on disk uses PR-NN.md. On Linux (case-sensitive fs, confirmed by env) the lowercase glob matches ZERO files, so nodes=[], topo=[], critical_path=[], and run() returns {ok:true} — a silent empty-DAG. _parse_pr lowercases the stem only AFTER globbing, so its normalization never compensates for the glob miss. (DAG-SPEC.md:210 documents the convention as lowercase pr-NNN.md, but the actual producers all write uppercase — a doc-vs-reality split that plan_dag follows on the wrong side.) This is distinct from super-polish #38 (topo_sort double-count on duplicate ids); #38 left line 145's glob untouched.
confirmed_findings.fix: Make discovery case-insensitive, e.g. pr_files = sorted(p for p in prs_dir.glob('[pP][rR]-*.md') if not p.stem.startswith('_')). (Or normalize: iterate prs_dir.glob('*.md') and keep p whose stem.lower() starts with 'pr-'.)

confirmed_findings.title: pr-review Phase 9 never flips PR status in 02-prs.md: REPLACE pattern '{pr-id}.*status.*' is a single-line regex that can't span the header/Status lines
confirmed_findings.mechanism: The canonical 02-prs.md (code-dev-plan.md:309-316) puts the PR id on the header line ('## PR-001 — title') and the status on a SEPARATE line ('- **Status:** not-started'). Line 485 does REPLACE(prs-content, pattern="{pr-id}.*status.*", replacement=...). Regex '.' does not cross newlines by default, so no single line contains both 'PR-001' and 'status'; the pattern matches nothing. (It is also case-wrong: the file has '**Status:**' capital-S vs the lowercase 'status' in the pattern.) prs-updated therefore equals prs-content, and line 486 re-writes the file unchanged — the '✅ ready to push' status is never recorded. Same family as #40 but a distinct site (pr-review P9, not pr-create) and a distinct mechanism (cross-line/case-mismatched regex).
confirmed_findings.fix: Operate on the PR's section like pr-create should: extract the '## PR-001 …' block, REPLACE the '- **Status:** <old>' line within that block, write the block back. Or use a multiline/section-scoped replace anchored to the Status line (capital S), not a single-line '{pr-id}.*status.*' regex.

confirmed_findings.title: journal-log references unbound pr-id on 'general' (non-PR) entries — shadow finding text and the human test-action hint interpolate an undefined variable
confirmed_findings.mechanism: pr-id is bound ONLY inside the IF pr-target ≠ "none" block (lines 119-121). The shadow-update loop runs unconditionally whenever files-changed ≠ "skip" (line 160, independent of pr-target), and line 166 builds finding-text ← "Modified during {pr-id} · …" — referencing pr-id which is unbound when the user logged a 'general' entry. The author guarded the other consumers with the '| general' fallback (lines 183, 211, 257) but missed line 166 and line 310 ('ctest -R {pr-id}'). Same unbound-variable class as super-polish #9/#10 but at sites those findings didn't cover.
confirmed_findings.fix: Use the same fallback already used elsewhere: bind pr-id ← "PR-{pad(pr-target,3)}" when pr-target≠'none' else 'general' once near line 117-122, OR change lines 166 and 310 to {pr-id | 'general'}.

confirmed_findings.title: code-dev thaw restores the PROJECT _meta.md workflow-step to the PHASE's prior value, corrupting project state whenever the two differed (the normal case)
confirmed_findings.mechanism: At freeze, the project and phase _meta.md hold independent workflow-step values (real example: project axon-architecture has 'workflow-step:   study' while a started phase has 'workflow-step:  build'). Freeze snapshots each correctly: phase → 'frozen  # was: {phase prior}' (line 63), project → 'frozen  # was: {project prior}' (line 65). But thaw parses a single prior from the PHASE meta only (line 78: prior ← EXTRACT(phase-meta.workflow-step, 'was: (\S+)')), then at line 81-82 restores the PROJECT meta to that same phase-derived prior: REPLACE(proj-dir/_meta.md, 'workflow-step:   frozen', 'workflow-step:   {prior}'). So a project that was 'study' before freeze comes back as 'build' (the phase's value) after thaw. Secondary defect: the project REPLACE pattern 'workflow-step:   frozen' is a substring of what freeze wrote ('frozen  # was: study'), so it also leaves a dangling '# was: study' comment.
confirmed_findings.fix: Parse and restore the project's own prior separately: project-prior ← EXTRACT(proj-meta.workflow-step, 'was: (\S+)'), and REPLACE the whole frozen line including its comment, e.g. REPLACE(proj-dir/_meta.md, 'workflow-step:   frozen  # was: {project-prior}', 'workflow-step:   {project-prior}'). Keep the phase restore using the phase-derived prior.

confirmed_findings.title: advance-guard reads empty W:_workflow-run-path under `--name` invocation — every `--name` fixed-mode run crashes at the first transition
confirmed_findings.mechanism: RESOLVE PATH (lines 55-57) resolves the workflow file into the LOCAL variable `path` (`IF path ≡ ∅ AND name ≠ ∅ → path ← "workspace/workflows/{name}.yml"`) and never STOREs it back to W:_workflow-run-path. The documented `--name` form (HELP line 34) binds only W:_workflow-run-name (flag-binding convention stated at line 61: each flag → W:_<prog>-<flag>, separate keys), so W:_workflow-run-path stays ∅. Validate/load correctly use the local `{path}` (lines 73, 76), but the in-loop advance-guard at line 179 dereferences the wrong symbol: `advance, "--workflow {W:_workflow-run-path} --from {cursor.id} ..."`. Under `--name`, that interpolates `--workflow ""` → workflow_run.py `_load("")` (tools/workflow_run.py:157-160) raises OSError, which the CLI's try/except only catches for WorkflowJumpError (workflow_run.py:263) → uncaught traceback. The advance is inside the LOOP (line 104) and fires unconditionally in fixed mode, so the run dies at the first on-complete transition.
confirmed_findings.fix: Use the resolved local variable, not the W key: change line 179 to `--workflow {path}`. (And/or STORE(W:_workflow-run-path, path) right after line 57 so any later W-keyed reference stays consistent.)

confirmed_findings.title: advance trajectory skip-check is broken by a name/id field mismatch — record-step stores the synapse NAME in `node`, advance compares it to cursor.id
confirmed_findings.mechanism: The promote-fix deliberately made the run record the synapse NAME: workflow-run.md line 120 is `record-step --node {cursor.name}` (comment lines 116-119 explain it must be the name so promote stays executable), so each trajectory step's `node` field holds e.g. 'code-dev-study'. But advance()'s skip-detection (workflow_run.py:79) does `elif last != cursor_id:` where `last = _last_node(trajectory)` returns the `node` field (the NAME) and `cursor_id` is the graph ID (e.g. 's1'). For any real workflow (name ≠ id, which is universal — ids are s1/s2, names are program names), `last != cursor_id` is ALWAYS true, so advance raises WorkflowJumpError('out-of-order advance ...') on the very first transition whenever a trajectory is supplied. This breaks the documented CLI path `advance --run-id` (CLI help line 219: 'check --from against this run's recorded trajectory'). Compounding it, the in-program guard at workflow-run.md:179 never passes --run-id, so the only part of the guard 'with real teeth against a skip' (advance docstring, lines 64-66) is simultaneously dead in-program AND broken on the CLI path. The two same-campaign fixes (record-the-name for promote; check-cursor-against-trajectory for skips) are mutually incompatible.
confirmed_findings.fix: Make the skip-check compare like-for-like. Either record the id alongside and compare ids (have _last_node read a dedicated id field), or in advance() resolve cursor_id→name via synapses_by_id and compare the trajectory `node` (name) to that name. Simplest: store BOTH in record_step (it already accepts name) and have _last_node prefer the id field for the advance comparison while promote keeps using name.

confirmed_findings.title: W:ws-path is never set, so workflow-run writes the run trajectory outside the workspace tree (record-step/promote --workspace resolves to empty)
confirmed_findings.mechanism: Both the per-step record (`record-step ... --workspace {W:ws-path}`, line 120) and the promote path (`promote ... --workspace {W:ws-path}`, line 47) interpolate W:ws-path, but W:ws-path is never STOREd anywhere in workspace/ or tools/ (grep for STORE(W:ws-path)/W:ws-path← returns nothing — only RETRIEVE sites exist). So `{W:ws-path}` expands to empty and the CLI receives `--workspace ''`. workflow_run.py _traj_path (line 171) then builds os.path.join('', 'memory', 'episodic', 'workflow-traj', f'{run}.json') → a CWD-relative `memory/episodic/workflow-traj/...`, NOT `workspace/memory/...` (the CLI default is 'workspace', workflow_run.py:233). The trajectory is written to the wrong place; any tooling that reads `workspace/memory/episodic/workflow-traj/` (and the promote MODE invoked as a separate process/turn, which also reads the mis-set path) sees nothing there. The recording silently lands off-tree.
confirmed_findings.fix: Resolve a concrete workspace root before these calls — e.g. STORE/derive ws-path = the workspace dir (the same value the CLI default 'workspace' uses) and pass it, or drop --workspace so the tool's own default ('workspace') applies. Ensure record-step and promote use the identical, real workspace root.

confirmed_findings.title: workflow-new resume loses all built state: PHASE-C resets synapses to [] and resume into PHASE-D/E reads unhydrated locals, writing a corrupt/empty workflow
confirmed_findings.mechanism: workflow-new advertises a resumable dialog (lines 41, 105 persist synapses into W:_workflow-author-state). RESUME-IF-PRESENT (line 47) does `GOTO(SECTION:PHASE-{state.phase})`. But (a) PHASE-C entry unconditionally re-initializes `synapses ← []` (line 83), discarding state.synapses that line 105 carefully accumulated — a resume mid-build erases prior steps; and (b) resuming to PHASE-D/PHASE-E jumps past PHASE-A/B/C, yet PHASE-E (lines 126-133) builds the draft from LOCAL variables name/domain/mode/goal/synapses/triggers/allow-sug/allow-dev, none of which are rehydrated from `state` on the resume path — they are all ∅, so WRITE-YAML emits a draft with name:∅ and synapses:∅ (then start: (synapses[0].id | "s1") dereferences an empty list). Additionally line 78 uses `APPEND(W:_workflow-author-state, {phase:"C", goal, synapses:[]})` where every other state write is STORE (lines 51, 64); APPEND on the dict-valued state key is the wrong operator and corrupts the state shape (phase/name may not survive for a later resume).
confirmed_findings.fix: On resume, rehydrate locals from state before the GOTO (name/domain/mode/goal/synapses/triggers/allow-* ← state.*), and make PHASE-C seed `synapses ← (state.synapses | [])` instead of always []. Change line 78 APPEND to STORE (merging into, not appending to, the dict state) so phase/name/goal/synapses persist coherently across a pause.

confirmed_findings.title: R_MEMORY_RESPECTED's manifest-locked BLOCK silently degrades to WARN in the crucible change-set gate unless invoked from exactly the repo root (CWD-fragile activation flag)
confirmed_findings.mechanism: The C10 fix re-activated R_MEMORY_RESPECTED in run_changeset() by calling it per changed file: `r_memory_respected.check({"program_text": text, "program_path": p, "state": {}})` (crucible.py:176). It passes `state: {}` — no `workspace_path` and no `repo_root`. R_MEMORY_RESPECTED._is_required() (r_memory_respected.py:38-54) therefore falls through to `workspace = state.get("workspace_path", "workspace")` and reads the activation flag from the RELATIVE path `workspace/memory/longterm/memory-respected-required.md`, which only resolves when os.getcwd() is exactly the repo root. The three sibling change-set rules in the SAME run_changeset loop (r_dont_do, r_no_orphan_tools, r_workflow_node_order) instead receive `ctx={..., "repo_root": cwd}` and resolve their flags from `ctx['repo_root']` (CWD-robust). So R_MEMORY_RESPECTED is the odd one out: when an operator sets L:memory-respected-required=true expecting the manifest-declared BLOCK (manifest.py:40 lists it as a BLOCK-tier crucible rule), the rule still emits only WARN — and the change-set gate passes — whenever `crucible.py changeset` runs from any CWD other than the repo root. This is the same fail-open class (a manifest-locked BLOCK silently inert) that the C10 fix was meant to close.
confirmed_findings.fix: Make R_MEMORY_RESPECTED's flag resolution CWD-independent like its siblings: at crucible.py:176 pass `"state": {"workspace_path": os.path.join(cwd, "workspace")}` (cwd is the repo root used everywhere else in run_changeset), OR add a `repo_root` fallback inside r_memory_respected._is_required (mirror r_no_orphan_tools._required which reads `os.path.join(ctx.get('repo_root','.'), 'workspace', 'memory', 'longterm', ...)`). Either makes the manifest-locked BLOCK fire regardless of invocation CWD.

confirmed_findings.title: crucible change-set gate returns zero violations (fail-open) when the diff base cannot be resolved — single-commit repo or missing origin/main
confirmed_findings.mechanism: changed_files() computes the base as `git merge-base HEAD origin/main 2>/dev/null || git rev-parse HEAD~1`. On a repo with no origin/main AND no second commit, `git rev-parse HEAD~1` prints the literal unresolved string 'HEAD~1' to stdout (exit 128, error to stderr), so base becomes the literal 'HEAD~1'. The subsequent `git diff --name-status HEAD~1...HEAD` then fails (invalid revision) and is swallowed by the bare `except Exception: return []` (lines 137-138). With changed_files==[], EVERY change-set rule (r_new_needs_test, r_dont_do, r_no_orphan_tools, r_workflow_node_order, and the r_memory_respected per-file loop) early-returns None ('no diff -> dormant'), so `crucible changeset` reports {ok:true, violations:[]} — the entire static change-set gate passes vacuously. It is fail-open rather than fail-closed: a degenerate/un-resolvable git state should refuse, not green-light.
confirmed_findings.fix: Distinguish 'no changes' from 'could not compute changes'. In changed_files, if the resolved base is empty or still equals the literal unresolved token (e.g. 'HEAD~1' when rev-parse failed), or the `git diff` invocation returns non-zero, return a sentinel that run_changeset treats as fail-closed (ok:false, reason 'diff base unresolved') rather than an empty list that reads as 'clean'.

assessments.dimension: PROMISE vs MECHANICAL REALITY (merged main, cc2744e)
assessments.summary: AXON's hardest claim — axon/ write-protection (R9) — genuinely HOLDS with triple-redundant mechanical enforcement (in-process R9WriteError, enforce.py CLI exit 1, PreToolUse hook exit 2), all verified live. The other four marquee claims (identity unconditional, coherence pre-render, rules mechanical, fail-loud) are mechanically COMPUTED but only ADVISORILY enforced per-turn, because the sole per-turn output surface (the Stop hook) is LOG-ONLY by deliberate design. The kernel's own "Enforcement reality" note (KERNEL-SLIM lines 89-95) honestly concedes most of this gap, though it is now slightly stale re: the now-installed PreToolUse write-gate.

points.claim: Identity unconditional — coherence guardian / R_COHERENCE blocks persona-bleed on every output, no flag required
points.evidence: tools/rules/r_coherence.py: declared severity=BLOCK, ZERO activation-flag gating (grep 'required' count=0), so the RULE is unconditional. Live-tested: verify.py output --text 'As an AI, I think...' → passed:false, R_COHERENCE BLOCK. BUT the only per-turn surface running it is the Stop hook (verify_stop.py), which is LOG-ONLY (stderr + episodic jsonl, always sys.exit(0) — docstring: 'a Stop hook cannot un-send'). So the verdict is computed and surfaced, never actually blocks a render in the live Claude Code session. Hard block exists only at the crucible merge gate (off the per-turn path) and via model discipline. Separately, identity-disclosure keys host-harness/host-model/disclose-execution-layer all read <absent> on disk this session, so the identity gate correctly falls back to minimal AXON render (fail-silent as designed) — disclosure-from-declared-keys-only HOLDS, but the keys are not populated.

points.claim: Memory protected — programs may NEVER write axon/; memory routed through the single API
points.evidence: axon/-integrity protection is genuinely hard and triple-wired. Live-tested _axon_io.atomic_write('axon/__probe__') → raises R9WriteError BEFORE touching disk (actor=unset, not in _R9_WHITELIST={'loop-receipt'}). enforce.py check-write --target axon/KERNEL-SLIM.md → exits 1 with allowed:false. PreToolUse hook (enforce_pretooluse.py) maps Write/Edit to enforce.py and exits 2 (deny) on failure. All three read dev-mode via the single canonical _longterm reader from the SAME workspace longterm dir (F08/F18/F39/F46 split-brain fix verified — default_workspace()=/.../axon/workspace, dev-mode='false'). This is the one marquee claim where 'enforced' really does mean 'cannot be bypassed by a program.' NOTE the weaker sibling: R_MEMORY_RESPECTED (memory-API routing) is WARN-default, BLOCK only if memory-respected-required=true (absent on disk), and per manifest.py runs ONLY in crucible/lint/audit — NOT the verify runtime gate — so that narrower 'single-memory-API' sense is advisory.

points.claim: Coherence is proactive — guardian scans every output BEFORE render; persona-bleed = HALT + rewrite
points.evidence: The temporal claim ('before render', 'scans every output before render', KERNEL-SLIM line 47/164) is FALSE as wired. The only per-turn coherence surface is the Stop hook, which fires AFTER the message is rendered and is LOG-ONLY by explicit design (verify_stop.py: 'a Stop hook cannot un-send, and exit-2-blocking would risk bricking a session on a false positive, so the crucible MERGE gate is where rules BLOCK'). No PreToolUse/pre-render hook gates assistant text. So coherence is post-hoc advisory in the live session, not proactive/pre-render. The rule logic is BLOCK-capable and correct; the wiring cannot stop a render.

points.claim: Rules are mechanical — kernel rules become mechanical checks, not behavioral guidance
points.evidence: TRUE at compile/merge: crucible.py is fail-closed (errored/unknown control counts as BLOCK failure; run_changeset runs static rules over the changeset with real exit codes), and manifest.py + test_rules_manifest lock the 4 runner lists so a rule can't silently go dead. PARTIAL at runtime: verify.py status shows 19 registered rules, but the runtime gate is invoked ONLY by the LOG-ONLY Stop hook. Of the runtime BLOCK rules, R_COHERENCE/R_DRIFT_GATE/R9(action) need no flag and R_STATE_SURFACED is live (state-surfaced-required=true + active-phase 'axon-workflow-discipline:3-pr' → BLOCK confirmed), but R_REASONING_TRACE, R_GROUNDED_CLAIMS, R_ADVERSARY_SCAN are dormant (flags absent), and R_IDENTITY_LOCK/R_MEMORY_RESPECTED/R_PROJECT_ANCHOR are WARN-default. So runtime = 'mechanically computed, advisorily surfaced.' Verifier correctly reports warn_is_block:false (F15/F16 — WARN never fails the gate even under strict halt-mode).

points.claim: Fail loud — on failure FAIL(program,reason): loud, logged, recoverable; no silent fallback
points.evidence: Strong where a real chokepoint exists: R9WriteError is a raised exception with an actionable message; enforce.py emits a structured refusal envelope; PreToolUse denies with stderr text + exit 2; crucible fails closed. WEAKER at the response gate: a BLOCK verdict from verify_stop.py is written to stderr (truncated 1500 chars) + appended to memory/episodic/response-gate-log.jsonl, then exits 0 — 'loud' only to whoever reads hook stderr/logs, and invisible to the user as an actual block. So fail-loud HOLDS for writes/merge, PARTIAL for per-turn output (logged-but-not-halted).

points.claim: Kernel's own hardness claims match the wiring (self-honesty)
points.evidence: KERNEL-SLIM lines 89-95 ('Enforcement reality') are admirably honest: they state the response gate runs by 'AGENT DISCIPLINE (advisory), not mechanically' until the Stop-hook wrapper + L:*-required flags are active, and tell the reader to run verify.py status and NOT read 'enforced' as 'cannot be bypassed.' Accurate for the response gate. STALE in two spots: (1) the same note hedges the write-gate '...while L:host-cap-enforce is self with no PreToolUse hook' — but enforce_pretooluse.py IS now installed and active in .claude/settings.json (the .proposed file is already promoted), so the axon/ write-gate DOES bite mechanically now; (2) workspace/harness/claude-code.md still declares STORE(L:host-cap-enforce,'self') target 'pretooluse-hook → enforce.py', now stale since that hook exists. Net: the kernel under-claims its write-gate and accurately self-reports the advisory response gate.

assessments.dimension: Functional verification of AXON's ACTIVE tool surface on merged main
assessments.summary: Sampled ~45 ACTIVE tools across every category (kernel, os, integration, synapse, system, audit, meta, viz, memory) by invoking --help plus a real read-only subcommand on each. The surface is healthy: every priority recently-fixed tool (dag, phase-model, workflow-runner, cron, dispatch, verify, crucible, autonomous-mode, pr_*) works correctly with real output, the registry-driven health probe reports 135 ACTIVE / 0 FAILED, and registry-drift shows 147/147 zero drift. One genuine defect surfaced: docgen_verify crashes with a ValueError traceback when given a relative --workspace path (works fine with an absolute path) — the recurring CWD/relative-path bug class this repo has been fighting.

points.claim: dag (system) works — verify + render machinery is live
points.evidence: `python3 tools/dag.py --help` lists 13 subcommands; `dag verify --all` -> `[]` (no DAG files = clean, exit 0); `synapse-infer --tool dag` emits a full neuron-contract record.

points.claim: phase-model (kernel) works — data-driven phase manifest load/render/status
points.evidence: On a temp project dir: `phase_model.py load --project <tmp>` seeds + returns the ordered ladder; `render` returns phases ordered by `order`; `status --project <tmp> --phase study` -> `{"status": null}` (queries cleanly). Exit 0.

points.claim: workflow-runner (kernel) works — deterministic traversal guard enforces declared edges
points.evidence: On workspace/workflows/adaptive-free-text.yml: `next-allowed --node s1` -> `{"allowed":["s3","s2"]}`; legal `advance --from s1 --to s3` -> `{"ok":true,"next":"s3"}` exit 0; illegal `advance --from s1 --to s99` -> `{"ok":false,"error":"illegal node-jump..."}` exit 1. Exactly the documented behavior.

points.claim: cron (kernel) works — scheduler lists real jobs
points.evidence: `cron.py list --workspace workspace` returns JSON with 11 jobs (health, igap-report, compile-rank, freshness, etc.) incl schedule_parsed + consecutive_failures fields. Exit 0.

points.claim: dispatch (kernel) works — TF-IDF match + stats
points.evidence: `dispatch.py stats` -> `{dispatch_index_count:1, current_threshold:0.65,...}`; `dispatch.py match --query 'compile a workflow'` -> real verdict `{action:fallback, best_match:{program:foo,confidence:0.0}}` exit 0.

points.claim: verify (kernel) works — rule verifier lists rules + enforcement posture
points.evidence: `verify.py rules` -> 19 registered rules (R3/R7/R9/... with phase+severity+module); `verify.py status` -> `{halt_mode:strict, warn_is_block:false, activation_flags_on_disk:{...}}`. Exit 0.

points.claim: crucible (kernel) works — control+test gate enumerates and runs all controls
points.evidence: `crucible.py list` -> 22 controls (pytest, changeset-rules, registry-drift, liveness...); `crucible.py status` actually RUNS them -> verdict `{passed:true, blocking_failures:[], warnings:[freshness], total:22}`. Exit 0.

points.claim: autonomous-mode (kernel) works — grant status + op authorization
points.evidence: `autonomous_mode.py status` -> active grant for artur.castiel-tno/axon with ops/deny lists; `check --op commit --repo ...` -> `{authorized:true}`; `check --op kernel-change` -> `{authorized:false, reason:'inviolable floor'}`. Correct allow/deny. Exit 0.

points.claim: pr_* family (os) works — aggregate/sync/drift/export
points.evidence: All four `--help` print correct usage exit 0; `pr_aggregate.py --all-projects --json` exits 0 (empty set = no open PR lists in tracked projects, not a failure).

points.claim: docgen_verify (audit) is PARTIAL — crashes on a relative --workspace path
points.evidence: `docgen_verify.py --workspace workspace` -> uncaught `ValueError: 'workspace/AXON-DOCS-CHEATSHEET.md' is not in the subpath of '<abs axon root>'` at line 94 (`doc.relative_to(ROOT)`), exit 1 with 2 ValueError lines. Same call with an ABSOLUTE path runs clean and correctly reports the real violation (doc references missing programs/code-dev-tour.md). Bug only triggers when a violation exists AND the arg is relative.

points.claim: freshness (meta) works — correctly reports a real docs-staleness defect (not a tool bug)
points.evidence: `freshness.py check` -> `{ok:false, checks:{...docgen_verify:false...}}` exit 1; the false sub-check is the genuine missing-cross-ref (code-dev-tour.md). freshness invokes docgen_verify via its own CWD-relative default which works; the failure is real drift it is designed to surface.

points.claim: synapse + predicate + goal (synapse) work — predicate language + goal store + ranker stats
points.evidence: `predicate.py eval --expr '1 == 1' --ctx '{}'` -> `{result:true}`; `goal.py list` -> `{ok:true, count:0, goals:[]}`; `synapse_suggest.py stats` -> `{predicate_errors:0}`. Exit 0.

points.claim: synapse-infer / synapse-validate / domain_validate (system) work
points.evidence: `synapse_infer.py --tool dag` emits full v1.1 neuron-contract JSON; `domain_validate.py --all --json` -> `{ok:true, count:2, valid:2, invalid:0}`. Help clean for synapse_validate. Exit 0.

points.claim: registry-drift / doc-counts / axon-state / loop-receipt (kernel) work
points.evidence: `registry_drift.py check` -> `{registered:147, on_disk:147, drift_count:0}`; `doc_counts.py counts` -> `{programs:158, tools:147, rules:31}`; `axon_state.py summary` -> per-log summary; `loop_receipt.py list` -> a real committed two-phase receipt. Exit 0.

points.claim: call_graph / coherence-lint / liveness (audit/meta) work
points.evidence: `call_graph.py --workspace workspace` -> `{ok:true, nodes:13, cycles:[], longest_path:4}`; `coherence_lint.py check` -> `{ok:true, defect_count:0}`; `liveness.py --help` lists report/check. Exit 0.

points.claim: integration tools work — reservoir-pvt, mcp-client, library, axon-bridge
points.evidence: `reservoir_pvt.py --api 35 --gor 600 --temp 200 --steps 3` computes a real Standing PVT table (oil_sg, Pb, Rs, Bo); `mcp_client.py list-servers` -> 1 server (pyrestoolbox, 108 tools); library + axon-bridge `--help` clean. Exit 0.

points.claim: project-graph (viz) works — generates a real DAG + viewer
points.evidence: `project_graph.py --out <tmp>` -> `{ok:true, nodes:60, edges:1, by_location:{...}}` and writes graph.json (20KB) + viewer.html (17KB). Exit 0.

points.claim: memory + os utilities work — memory, calculator, tokenizer, clock, events, deps, test-runner, deprecation-log, workflow-dag, aegis-policy, phase-gate
points.evidence: memory list (9 L: keys); calculator '2+2*10' -> 22; tokenizer --text -> 4 tokens; clock -> NTP ISO ts; events hook-list -> {count:0}; deps tree --program code-dev -> full tree; test-runner list -> real corpus; deprecation-log cron -> {overdue:0,imminent:0}; workflow-dag analyze --dir -> {ok:true, synapses:3, cycles:1}; aegis-policy resolve --capability develop -> {allowed:false, reason:'policy: human'} (fail-closed). All exit 0.

points.claim: Registry-driven health probe corroborates the surface is wired and reachable
points.evidence: `health.py --include-kernel --workspace workspace` -> `{ACTIVE:135, FAILED:0, SKIPPED:12, score:100}`; the 12 SKIPPED are all OPTIONAL/deprecated shims (a2a, axiom, axon-eval, hooks, rtk, compile-write/suggest/optimizer, memory-sync, skill-adapter, migrate-synapse-blocks, axon-managed). No ACTIVE tool failed its probe.

points.claim: No dead tools found in the sample — every ACTIVE script exists and is invocable
points.evidence: registry_drift reports 0 drift (147 registered == 147 on disk, no registry-orphan/filesystem-orphan); every tool I invoked dispatched and ran (the only exit-2s were my own wrong arg names, after which the tools printed correct usage and ran on retry).

assessments.dimension: Promised-but-not-delivered / incomplete / tech-debt on merged main
assessments.summary: Main carries a moderate, well-documented debt load: most gaps are deliberately deferred and visibly marked (STUB programs, "deferred" doc notes, owner-gated proof), not silent rot. The two genuinely material items are (1) the axon-million proof being harness-ready but never run — the headline claim is unproven — and (2) compiled .cmp.md artifacts having only a structural freshness check with byte-identical regen explicitly deferred, so they can silently drift from source (and several .cmp.md have no source at all). The rest is housekeeping debt (orphan stubs, quarantined compileds, un-finished PR-142 deprecation scrub) that is tracked but accumulating across releases.

points.claim: axon-million proof is owner-gated: harness-ready but NEVER actually run — the headline H1 claim ('AXON beats bare model') has zero collected results. benchmark/reports/ holds only .gitkeep; tools/dual_agent_eval.py cmd_run_mms returns status:'harness-ready' with no --backend, and a real run needs an owner-supplied ANTHROPIC_API_KEY / Claude subscription.
points.evidence: benchmark/reports/ contains only .gitkeep (0-byte); tools/dual_agent_eval.py:710 'demo backend emits no solver code, so MMS goals cannot pass — a real coding model is required for the proof'; pre-registration benchmark/preregistrations/prereg-2026-05-28-*.md is LOCKED but unexecuted; CHANGELOG 3.7.0: 'Production telemetry for hit-rate — baseline not yet collected'.

points.claim: Per-turn response gate (verify_stop Stop hook) is held LOG-ONLY by design: on a violation it writes stderr + an episodic log and ALWAYS exit 0. Escalation to a hard exit-2 BLOCK is an owner switch, and zero false-positive data has been logged yet, so flipping it is blind today.
points.evidence: tools/hooks/verify_stop.py:99-102 'LOG-ONLY ... Escalate this to exit 2 only after validating zero false positives on real AXON output'; .claude/settings.json _comment confirms 'verify_stop is LOG-ONLY'. Matches the memory note (only open held item).

points.claim: Compiled .cmp.md staleness is not truly guarded: the byte-identical regen-and-diff CI gate is explicitly DEFERRED. Headers carry a wall-clock 'Compiled:' timestamp and NO source content-hash, so a compiled artifact can silently drift from its edited source — only a structural freshness check (header fields present, dir populated) exists.
points.evidence: workspace/AXON-DOCS-COMPILER.md:65 'A byte-identical regen-and-diff CI gate is **deferred** ... Tracked for a future PR'; .cmp.md headers (e.g. code-dev.cmp.md) show 'Compiled: 2026-05-04' with no src-hash field; tests/test_compiled_freshness.py only checks structure, not content equivalence.

points.claim: Multiple compiled .cmp.md artifacts have NO corresponding source program — they are stale orphans of deleted/renamed programs (e.g. chat-folder, dev-new, interactive, list-chats, new-chat, switch-chat, plan-add/-done/-list/-new, register-preference).
points.evidence: workspace/programs/compiled/ contains chat-folder.cmp.md, dev-new.cmp.md, interactive.cmp.md, plan-done.cmp.md etc. with no matching workspace/programs/<name>.md (confirmed by source-existence sweep); these survive because the freshness gate never checks source presence.

points.claim: 16 STUB-status + 4 alias-stub programs remain on main. Three are orphan-stubs (code-dev-actions, code-dev-dry-run, code-dev-examples) referenced by other programs but never implemented — they only LOG(WARN) and print 'stub (not yet implemented)', explicitly logged for an axon-cleanup PR-119 follow-up that never landed.
points.evidence: workspace/programs/code-dev-actions.md:25 'LOG(WARN, ... stub only. See axon-cleanup PR-119.)'; code-dev-dry-run.md, code-dev-examples.md identical pattern; grep shows 16 'status: STUB' programs incl. several '(autogen-stub — needs description)' (code-dev-meta-board/-igap/-usage/-dispatch-stats, code-dev-rules-audit, code-dev-events-emit).

points.claim: Deprecated subsystem 'semantic-search' (removed in axon-cleanup wave 2, on the strict deprecation set) is still referenced — as commented-out 'deprecated PR-142' lines — in 8 workspace/programs/*.md. The PR-142 scrub never finished, and the deprecation guard does NOT catch it because the test only checks 4 A-tier docs (README/CONTRIBUTING/AGENTS/CHANGELOG) + pyproject, not B-tier program source.
points.evidence: grep 'semantic-search' workspace/programs/*.md = 17 lines across find-program.md, mode-router.md, meta.md, code-dev-init.md, code-dev-plan.md, list-tools.md, authoring-guide.md, igap-improve.md, all tagged '# deprecated (axon-cleanup PR-142)'; tests/test_no_stale_subsystems.py scopes only _A_TIER_DOCS — residue is un-guarded.

points.claim: Quarantined compiled programs (verbatim-copy ratio 1.00 + synapse-header-oversize after PR-108) are parked off-dispatch in _quarantine.md, tracked as axon-cleanup PR-121/PR-122 'recompile with synapse-aware bytes-budget logic' — which never landed.
points.evidence: workspace/programs/compiled/_quarantine.md:138-141 lists stats/status/turn-log/versions (verbatim) and code-dev-actions/-audit/-check-structure (synapse-header oversize) 'Off-dispatch until recompiled ... tracked as axon-cleanup PR-122'.

points.claim: Domain reviewer reservoir-review.md ships marked DOMAIN-REVIEW-PENDING: its engineering criteria (applicability ranges, concern order) are 'not yet engineer-validated', and the deterministic FLOOR checks it should enforce are deferred to authoritative neurons it only advisorily NOTEs.
points.evidence: workspace/programs/reservoir-review.md:41 'DOMAIN-REVIEW-PENDING: the engineering criteria below ... ' and :102 '(DOMAIN-REVIEW-PENDING — criteria not yet engineer-validated.)'; :62 'FLOOR (deferred — deterministic neurons OWN these; this reader never blocks)'.

points.claim: Synapse ranker weights are static / un-tuned: the auto-improve loop that would learn weights from production hit-rate is deferred (PR-130-132 in 3.7.0 scorecard; PR-152 'auto-improve will tune the weight set'). The hit-rate counter is live but its baseline was never collected, so there is no data to tune against.
points.evidence: CHANGELOG.md:39 '⬛ Auto-improve loop (PR-130-132) — deferred to a future dev-project'; workspace/tools/synapse-suggest.md:117 'PR-152 auto-improve will tune the weight set'; CHANGELOG.md:38 'counter live; baseline not yet collected'.

points.claim: Outbound MCP capability is v1 stdio-transport-only; HTTP/SSE transport and the reverse direction (AXON exposing itself as an MCP server) are deferred. The shared mcp-servers.json pyrestoolbox entry is a disabled placeholder template requiring manual local wiring.
points.evidence: tools/mcp_client.py:13 'Scope (v1): stdio transport only ... HTTP/SSE transport and an MCP[-server] ... '; tools/REGISTRY.json:208 'stdio transport only in v1; HTTP + MCP-server (reverse) deferred'; workspace/mcp-servers.json:23 enabled:false placeholder <PYRESTOOLBOX_MCP_DIR>.

points.claim: Reservoir objective-function built-in module is deferred to Phase 2: the MVP evaluates the objective externally from exported rates; several OBJECTIVE-FUNCTION-INTERFACE open questions (time-varying prices, multi-phase injection adjoint sign) are 'not needed for MVP'.
points.evidence: workspace/OBJECTIVE-FUNCTION-INTERFACE.md:322 'The built-in module can be deferred to Phase 2'; Section 8 Open Questions list time-varying prices / group objectives / injection adjoint as MVP-deferred.

points.claim: Accountability ledger surface (un-reconciled spawned/background work) is also LOG-ONLY advisory at turn-end — it reminds but never blocks, sharing the same non-enforcing Stop hook as the response gate.
points.evidence: tools/accountability.py:12 'The Stop hook surfaces any OPEN entries at turn-end (LOG-ONLY)'; tools/hooks/verify_stop.py:90 _surface_open_accountability '(LOG-ONLY, never blocks)'.

points.claim: DAG-SPEC PR-110 lowercase normalization is an open Phase-3 housekeeping task: the phase-2 DAG.json still stores uppercase PR-NNN ids, to be regenerated lowercase 'when PR-110 lands'.
points.evidence: workspace/DAG-SPEC.md:218 'this project's phases/2-design/03-prs/DAG.json will be regenerated in lowercase when PR-110 lands. Tracked as a Phase-3 housekeeping task.'

points.claim: F21 module-mode import conversion (python3 -m tools.x + flat->package rewrite) was deliberately NOT done — correctly so per the held-refactors decision (the 49 redundant bootstraps were removed; 3 functional sys.path inserts are intentionally KEPT). This is intentional non-debt, flagged so a future session does not naively 're-finish' it.
points.evidence: memory/axon-held-refactors.md: 'planned module-mode conversion ... was NOT done and is NOT needed'; tests/test_no_sys_path_bootstrap.py allowlists crucible.py/dont_do_lint.py/_axon_lib.py — the inserts are load-bearing because the editable axon install points at a different checkout.

points.claim: axon-million/proof-design.md (the proof decision log) is referenced by benchmark/METHODOLOGY.md but is NOT present in the repo — it lives only in the un-shipped local my-axon area, so a cloner cannot read the cited rationale.
points.evidence: benchmark/METHODOLOGY.md:9 'decision log: axon-million/proof-design.md'; find for 'axon-million' returns only that doc reference — no axon-million/ directory exists in the tree (same pattern as the gitignored my-axon/dev-projects audit files).

assessments.dimension: SAFETY posture: git-op rule rationalization (kernel v1.1.5) + dont-do enforcement
assessments.summary: The core safety invariants hold and are well-tested: kernel-change is structurally never delegable (denied before the grant is even loaded, and double-filtered at grant time), the destructive override is default-off, correctly scoped per-op-per-repo, and audited, and the floor is preserved by default with the kernel content-hash lock matching the merged file exactly. dont-do enforcement is largely coherent end-to-end (write-time token block + preflight change-set gate + capture-lint), with both rules wired into the crucible. Two real seams keep this from "strong": a delete-branch vs branch-delete naming collision where the same operation is gated oppositely depending on the string passed, and the fact that authorized() has no production caller (it is an advisory self-consult the agent must voluntarily run, not a mechanical interceptor) — so none of these git-op guarantees are actually enforced at the tool boundary.

points.claim: kernel-change is truly never delegable by any grant (inviolable floor)
points.evidence: tools/autonomous_mode.py:110-111 checks `op in ALWAYS_DENY` and returns authorized:False BEFORE load_grant() — no grant state can reach the kernel-change path. Defense-in-depth at grant time: grant_on (line 88) filters `--destructive` with `o in DESTRUCTIVE_OPS and o not in ALWAYS_DENY`, and `--ops` (line 84) drops anything in DENY_OPS (= ALWAYS_DENY + DESTRUCTIVE_OPS). Empirically: grant_on(repo, destructive=['kernel-change','force-push']) yields grant.destructive=['force-push'] (kernel-change stripped) and authorized(kernel-change) is False. test_kernel_change_never_delegable passes. KERNEL-SLIM.md:567,697 and workspace/autonomous-mode.md:21 both state it as the inviolable floor.

points.claim: the destructive override is default-OFF (the floor holds until an op is explicitly opted in)
points.evidence: grant_on default `destructive=None` -> `[o for o in (destructive or []) ...]` = [] (autonomous_mode.py:88). DEFAULT_OPS (line 32) = commit/push/pr-create/merge-squash/delete-branch — contains no force-push/reset-hard/amend/rebase. With a default safe grant, authorized(force-push) and authorized(reset-hard) are both False empirically. DEVELOPER.md changelog: 'No behaviour change by default — the destructive floor holds until an op is opted in.' test_destructive_denied_by_default passes.

points.claim: destructive delegation is correctly scoped per-op AND per-repo
points.evidence: authorized() (autonomous_mode.py:115-123): repo mismatch returns False (line 115-116); a destructive op is authorized only if `op in g['destructive']` (line 118) — a per-op list, not a blanket flag. Empirically: grant destructive=['force-push','reset-hard'] for a/b gives force-push@a/b=True, reset-hard@a/b=True, but force-push@x/y=False (other repo) and rebase@a/b=False (op not delegated). test_destructive_allowed_only_when_explicitly_delegated covers op-scope, repo-scope, and the wrong-op case.

points.claim: every grant and every authorization decision is audited
points.evidence: _audit appends ts+record to my-axon/memory/local/autonomous-mode-audit.jsonl (autonomous_mode.py:75-79). grant_on emits grant-on (line 92), grant_off emits grant-off (line 98), each successful ordinary authorize emits 'authorized' (line 126) and each destructive authorize emits 'authorized-destructive' (line 119). Verified: the audit jsonl contains grant-on (with destructive list), authorized, and authorized-destructive records after a test sequence. Note: DENY decisions are not audited (only granted ops are) — a minor observability gap, not a safety hole.

points.claim: the kernel-change rule is the same floor across kernel prose, the tool, and the doc; kernel content is locked
points.evidence: KERNEL-SLIM.md:697 (HARD RULE) + :567 (code-dev exception) + workspace/autonomous-mode.md:21-27 all state: reversible ops grant-delegable, destructive ops human-only unless explicitly per-op-per-repo delegated, kernel-file changes never delegable. test_kernel_version_lock.py pins v1.1.5 + sha256 baa26d9a2515...e16a2; recomputed hash of the merged axon/KERNEL-SLIM.md matches exactly, so the safety prose cannot drift silently.

points.claim: no bypass: a grant cannot smuggle a denied op in via --ops or junk values
points.evidence: grant_on filters --ops through `o not in DENY_OPS` (autonomous_mode.py:84) so force-push/kernel-change cannot enter grant.ops; --destructive is filtered to known DESTRUCTIVE_OPS minus ALWAYS_DENY (line 88) so junk strings ('rm -rf') are dropped. Empirically ops=['commit','kernel-change','force-push'] -> grant.ops=['commit']; destructive with junk -> only valid destructive ops kept. The authorized() destructive branch (line 117) intercepts a destructive op even if it somehow appeared in grant.ops, since it is checked against grant.destructive, not grant.ops.

points.claim: delete-branch vs branch-delete naming collision — the SAME operation is gated oppositely depending on the string the agent passes
points.evidence: DEFAULT_OPS includes 'delete-branch' (autonomous_mode.py:32, a safe default-granted op) while DESTRUCTIVE_OPS includes 'branch-delete' (line 30, human-only). They denote the same real git action (deleting a branch). Empirically with a default safe grant: authorized('delete-branch','a/b')=True but authorized('branch-delete','a/b')=False. So branch deletion — listed as destructive in the kernel (:697) and the doc (:21) — is in practice auto-authorized whenever the caller spells it 'delete-branch'. The docstring (line 12) and DEVELOPER.md call it 'branch-delete' as destructive, contradicting its presence in the safe DEFAULT_OPS set. Branch deletion is recoverable via reflog so impact is bounded, but the floor is not what the prose claims for this op.

points.claim: the git-op guarantees are mechanically enforced at the tool boundary (not just advisory)
points.evidence: authorized() has NO production caller: grep across tools/ shows it is invoked only by autonomous_mode.py's own CLI 'check' subcommand (line 170); enforce.py has no git-op handling (no force-push/reset-hard/merge-squash references). The protocol (workspace/autonomous-mode.md:38-43) says 'the agent MUST run autonomous-mode check ... and proceed only if authorized:true' — i.e. it relies on the agent voluntarily self-consulting before each git op. There is no PreToolUse-style interceptor on Bash git commands. So the kernel-change floor and destructive scoping are correct as a decision oracle but are advisory in practice; a non-compliant or confused agent that just runs `git push --force` is not mechanically blocked by this layer.

points.claim: dont-do is coherent end-to-end: tokenized rules block at write-time, prose/semantic caught at preflight, capture-gate forces tokenization (no silent prose escape)
points.evidence: Three legs are wired: (1) write-time hook (enforce_pretooluse.py) blocks tokenized match: hits at the keystroke via dont_do_violation, with exit 2 (PreToolUse deny) — and crucible.py:150-157 + registry.py:54-55 register both (2) R_DONT_DO (change-set token + fail-closed prose/semantic BLOCK, r_dont_do.py:115-128) and (3) R_DONT_DO_LINT (capture-gate, fails closed on prose-only prohibitions). All 103 related tests pass. SEAM: the write-time walk-up (enforce_pretooluse.py:60) and the active-project loader read _dont-do.md AND _dont-do-seeds.md AND phase _dont-do.md, but R_DONT_DO only reads <repo_root>/_dont-do.md (r_dont_do.py:64) and R_DONT_DO_LINT only lints files whose basename == '_dont-do.md' (r_dont_do_lint.py:53). So a PROSE-ONLY prohibition placed in a _dont-do-seeds.md (or a phase _dont-do.md outside repo-root) is not write-time-blockable (write-time only checks tokens) and is not caught by the capture-lint — confirmed: lint_text flags it but R_DONT_DO_LINT.check never feeds a seeds file to it. The 'every prohibition is ALWAYS enforced, no silently-un-enforced prose escape' claim in the hook docstring (lines 8-10) thus holds for repo-root _dont-do.md but has a narrow gap for seeds/phase files.

points.claim: the enforcement layers are fail-safe (a hook/rule fault must not block legitimate work, and dormant when no _dont-do exists)
points.evidence: Write-time dont_do_violation and _active_project_dont_dos wrap everything in try/except returning None/[] (enforce_pretooluse.py:118-119,150-152) so a fault never blocks a legit write; _axon_active() gates the whole hook to AXON-OS sessions only (line 177). R_DONT_DO/R_DONT_DO_LINT return None when no _dont-do.md or no changed_files (r_dont_do.py:90,98; r_dont_do_lint.py:43), so they are dormant in repos without the file. This is the correct fail-direction for the floor-preservation goal: default-off, opt-in.
